name: ai-order-suggestion
title: AI Order Suggestion
description: Review card for a caller-supplied proposed order, with structured fields, rationale, constraints, and a required acknowledgement that gates submission.

props.name: orderLabel
props.description: The proposal in one line, e.g. "Amoxicillin 500 mg oral, TDS, 5 days".

props.name: fields
props.description: { label, value, emphasis? } — the structured proposal, rendered verbatim. `emphasis` marks a field the reviewer most likely needs to change.

props.name: rationale
props.description: Why the order was proposed. Caller-supplied.

props.name: constraints
props.description: Cautions the reviewer must satisfy before signing — allergy status, renal dosing, unchecked interactions.

props.name: acknowledgementLabel
props.description: Overrides the default acknowledgement wording. It must remain an assumption of clinical responsibility, not a dismissal.

props.name: acknowledged
props.description: Controlled acknowledgement. Omit for internal state.

props.name: onSubmit
props.description: Caller-provided signing handler. Without it, submit stays disabled — the component cannot place an order.

props.name: canSubmit
props.description: Additional caller gate: authorisation, local protocol checks, an open encounter. Submit requires acknowledgement AND this.

props.name: actions
props.description: Secondary actions rendered beside submit: edit, discard, send to a prescriber.

props.name: limitations
props.description: Overrides the default limitations line passed to AiAnswerCard.

clinicalNotes: A generated order is the point where a UI stops describing and starts acting, so this component is designed so that it cannot act. It performs no dose calculation, no allergy check, no interaction screening, and no protocol validation — every field is rendered verbatim from the caller, and the card says so in its limitations. Submission is locked behind two independent gates: an explicit acknowledgement checkbox in which the reviewer states they have checked the proposal against the record and their local protocol and take clinical responsibility for it, and a caller-provided `onSubmit` handler. With no handler wired, the submit button is permanently disabled and the card says outright that it cannot place an order; `canSubmit` exists so the host can add authorisation or an open-encounter requirement on top. The acknowledgement is deliberately worded as an assumption of responsibility rather than a dismissal — a checkbox that reads "OK" trains people to click through, and one-click accept on a generated order is exactly the automation-bias failure that kills patients. The card is labelled "Not submitted" in both the provenance badge and beside the order line so that its state is never ambiguous in a screenshot or a handover. `constraints` is where the caller names what has NOT been checked — allergy status not asked, renal dosing unconfirmed, interactions unscreened — because the reviewer's real risk is assuming a check happened silently. Consumers must connect an authorised ordering workflow, local protocol checks, and audit logging explicitly; none of that lives here.
notes: Composes AiAnswerCard for the shell, checkbox + label for the acknowledgement gate, and button for the caller-owned submit. Acknowledgement supports controlled and uncontrolled use.
